data.title: UMS Integration Target Price Raised to S$1.73 From S$1.32 by UOB Kay Hian >558.SG
BHP Group outperforms peers after logging record copper and iron ore output
data.description:
** Shares of Australia's BHP rise as much as 2% to A$39.93

** Co reports FY copper production above 2 million metric tonnes (mt) for the first time ever, in line with Visible Alpha consensus estimates

** Posts record FY iron ore production of 290 Mt, at the upper end of its forecast range of 282-290 mt

** Posts Q4 iron ore output of 77.5 Mt, up 1% from the prior corresponding period, beating Visible Alpha consensus estimates

** Co flags increase to capital expenditure estimates for Jansen Stage 1 potash project in Canada to $7.0 billion-$7.4 billion from $5.7 billion

** Says first production from Jansen Stage 1 potash project pushed back to mid-2027 from previously targeted end-2026

** Shares of peers Rio Tinto and Fortescue up 1% and 1.4%, respectively

** Stock down 1.1% YTD, as of last close

data.title: Trump has already crossed Fed independence Rubicon: McGeever
data.description:
Whether Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell is fired next week, forced to resign in six months or allowed to muddle through to the end of his term next May, the supposedly sacrosanct notion of Fed independence has already been shattered.

Yet what's nearly as remarkable as President Donald Trump's attacks on Powell for not cutting interest rates is financial markets' resilience in the face of this extraordinary degree of political interference in monetary policy, unprecedented in recent decades.

Equity investors are known for being optimists, but today's Wall Street is veritably Teflon-coated.

Of course, Trump's attacks on Powell have not been without consequence. The dollar has clocked its worst start to a year since the United States dropped the gold standard in the early 1970s. Long-dated Treasury yields are the highest in 20 years, and the "term premium" on U.S. debt is the highest in over a decade.

Consumers' inflation expectations, by some measures, are also the highest in decades. Inflation has been above the Fed's 2% target for over four years, and the prospect of a dovish Fed under the stewardship of a new Trump-friendly Chair could keep it that way.

But that's not solely down to Fed policy and credibility risks. The Trump administration's fiscal and trade policies, and unilateralist position on the world political stage, have also tempted some investors to trim their exposure to U.S. debt and the dollar.

Still, Wall Street seems immune to all that, and it closed in the green on Wednesday after Trump played down a Bloomberg report that he will soon fire Powell, a step he says is "highly unlikely". Even at the point of maximum selling before that rebuttal, the big U.S. equity indices were down less than 1%.

Given the magnitude of the news investors were reacting to, that's barely a ripple, especially when you remember that the S&P 500 and Nasdaq hit record highs only 24 hours earlier.

Indeed, the S&P 500 is enjoying its third-fastest rebound from a 20% drawdown in history, according to Fidelity's Jurrien Timmer. Goldman Sachs analysts also note that the index's price-to-earnings ratio of 22 times forward earnings is in the 97th percentile since 1980. And the Nasdaq is up 40% in barely three months.

Taking all this into account, there's plenty of space for a correction. What's needed is a catalyst. Threatening the foundation of the financial system would seem to qualify, but will it?

BECOMING IMMUNE

One might argue that investors are simply skeptical that Trump really will oust Powell, even were it "for cause", ostensibly the Trump administration's ire over the $2.4 billion cost of renovating the Fed's building in Washington.

But Trump has made it clear for months that he wants Powell replaced by someone more malleable, so whether it happens in the coming weeks, months, or May next year, the new Fed Chair will almost certainly be someone strongly influenced by the president.

Of course, the Fed Chair is only one of 19 members of the Federal Open Market Committee and just one of 12 voting members at any given rate-setting meeting. He or she does not decide policy unilaterally. Still, the negative reaction to Powell leaving before his term is up could be powerful, even though you would expect it to be priced in to some extent by now.

All else being equal, a more dovish-leaning Fed will reasonably be expected to weigh on short-dated yields, steepen the yield curve, and weaken the dollar as bond investors price in more rate cuts, and keep inflation closer to 3% than 2%. In the short term, stocks could benefit from expectations of a lower policy rate, although higher long-dated yields would increase the discount rate, which could be particularly negative for Big Tech and other growth stocks.

JP Morgan CEO Jamie Dimon on Tuesday warned of the dangers of political interference in Fed policymaking, telling reporters on a conference call: "The independence of the Fed is absolutely critical. Playing around with the Fed can often have adverse consequences, absolutely opposite of what you might be hoping for."

That Rubicon has already been crossed, and for now at least, markets appear to have accepted that.

(The opinions expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters)

data.title: Salesforce CEO Marc Benioff Sells 2,250 Shares
data.description:
Reporter NameBenioff Marc
RelationshipChair and CEO
TypeSell
Amount$578,285
SEC FilingForm 4

Salesforce Chair and CEO, Marc Benioff, reported selling a total of 2,250 common stock shares on July 16, 2025, under a Rule 10b5-1 trading plan. The transactions occurred at weighted average prices ranging from $255.7397 to $258.5848, totaling $578,285 in sales. Post transactions, Benioff directly owns 11,911,571 shares and indirectly owns 10,107,000 shares through trusts and Marc Benioff Fund LLC. All shares were held directly or in the Marc R. Benioff Revocable Trust.

SEC Filing: Salesforce, Inc. [ CRM ] - Form 4 - Jul. 17, 2025

data.title: Nikkei Gains 0.2%, Led by Tech, Financial Stocks — Market Talk
data.description:
2014 ET - Japanese stocks are higher following signs of U.S. economic strength. Tech and financial stocks are leading gains. Nomura Research Institute is up 1.0% and Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group is 0.6% higher. USD/JPY is at 148.47, compared with 148.73 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments related to U.S. tariffs and domestic economic policy ahead of the Upper House elections on Sunday. The Nikkei Stock Average is up 0.2% at 39968.38. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

2010 ET - JGBs consolidate ahead of Japan's Upper House elections on Sunday. Surveys surrounding the elections show prospects of the Liberal Democratic Party-Komeito coalition retaining its majority have grown even more uncertain than earlier in the campaign, two members of Barclays' FICC Research say in a research report. "If the fiscally dovish opposition parties fare even better than projected in the pre-election polls, the initial post-election response would likely be an accelerated bear-steepening in JGBs," the members say. The 10-year JGB yield is steady at 1.555%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1942 ET - Japanese stocks may rise following signs of U.S. economic strength. Nikkei futures are up 0.4% at 40050 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 148.31, compared with 148.73 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments related to U.S. tariffs and domestic economic policy ahead of the Upper House election on Sunday. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 0.6% to 39901.19 on Thursday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1921 ET - Asked in an audience Q&A in Manhattan whether Fed governors are committed to maintaining their independence from political leaders, Fed Gov. Christopher Waller gives a strong affirmative. "I've never met a central banker in my life who didn't cherish it," Waller says of the principle that monetary authorities should get a free hand. Harsh criticism of the Fed from the White House has made central-bank independence a hot-button topic. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1904 ET - Fed governor Christopher Waller swiftly denies that anyone from the Trump administration has talked to him about succeeding Fed Chair Jerome Powell. Waller is speaking before a group of finance pros in Manhattan, laying out the case that the Fed should cut rates in July. In an audience Q&A, an attendee asks him if the Trump administration has approached him about Powell's job. "Nope!" he responds, and moves on to the next question. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1758 ET - Netflix isn't seeing any pain points in the economy from its end. Executives on the streaming service's 2Q investor call said while they're watching consumer sentiment in the broader economy, at this time there's nothing significant to note in the metrics and indicators it gets through its business. This comes as Netflix has cracked down on password sharing, built out an ad-supported offering and raised prices on some subscription tiers. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1745 ET - The venture capital industry's main trade group saluted the House passage of three cryptocurrency-related bills. "These bills will enhance the venture industry's ability to back transformative companies and drive lasting economic growth," said Bobby Franklin, president and CEO of National Venture Capital Association. He said the bills, GENIUS Act, the CLARITY Act, and the Anti-CBDC Surveillance State Act, establish a clear regulatory framework for stablecoins and other digital assets. Crypto startups globally raised $4.9 billion via 446 venture-capital deals in the first quarter of this year, according to research from Galaxy. Of those deals about 39% were for companies based in the U.S. ( yuliya.chernova@wsj.com )

1713 ET - U.S. home sales rose in June from May, marking five consecutive months of gains, RE/MAX Holdings reports. Chicago had the highest number of sales in June, jumping 12.9% from the same time last year, the report says. Buyers and sellers in the windy city's market have finally come to terms with the current housing landscape, RE/MAX premier broker Mike Opyd says. The mental shift has pushed more sellers to offer properties on the market for buyers to jump on before prices rise even further, Opyd says. (natalie.weger@wsj.com; @NatalieWeger)

1643 ET - Canadian consumer spending remained solid in 2Q, says Royal Bank of Canada economist Rachel Battaglia, based on anonymized cardholder data from the lender. This adds to a series of secondary and tertiary data that suggest the economy has not fallen off a cliff, as commentators and analysts feared due to President Trump's tariff threats. For the April-to-June period, core retail sales — which strips on automobiles and gasoline — rose 3%, based on activity on RBC credit and debit cards. Core retail sales did contract in June from May, according to RBC data, although Battaglia notes May was a particularly strong month. Quarterly spending growth remains positive in nearly all categories, Battaglia says, led by dining and entertainment. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1634 ET - Netflix can thank a weaker dollar for its raised outlook. The streaming service raised its revenue forecast to $44.8 billion to $45.2 billion for the year, adding that the majority of the increase is due to the recent depreciation of the dollar against most other currencies. Momentum in member growth and ad sales rounds out its higher guidance. Shares tick down 0.5% in after hours trading. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1612 ET - Bank of Nova Scotia's economics team reaffirms its call the Bank of Canada is done cutting rates for this year. Economic activity in Canada looks to be stronger — although still tepid — than what Scotiabank previously believed. It now believes Canada GDP in 2Q to be positive, versus an earlier call of a 1% contraction, citing strong labor data for June and three-straight months of higher existing-home sales. The Scotiabank economics team adds that informal conversations with bank clients suggest a more optimistic view of the economy relative to earlier this year. Scotiabank says the combination of "stronger, but not strong," growth and elevated core inflation will keep the BOC's policy rate steady at 2.75%. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1534 ET - Treasury yields rise as U.S. data indicate layoffs aren't picking up while retail sales remain robust. The indicators weaken the case for fast interest rate cuts by the Fed, as the Trump administration has advocated. The preliminary University of Michigan consumer survey for July, due tomorrow, is expected to tick higher in a WSJ poll, in another potential argument for a longer Fed hold. June housing starts are also forecast to accelerate. The 10-year rises 0.008 percentage point to 4.462% and the two-year increases 0.032 p.p. to 3.916%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: F&C Investment Trust Buys Back Over 32,000 Shares
data.description:
F&C Investment Trust repurchased 32,246 ordinary shares on Friday at an average price of 11.45 pounds sterling per share, to be held in treasury, according to a same-day filing with the New Zealand bourse.

The company's issued share capital consists of 480.6 million ordinary shares, including 81.2 million shares held in treasury, per the filing.

data.title: Netflix's profit surges. But it was not enough for the stock.
data.description:
By Lukas I. Alpert

Streaming giant's earnings come ahead of expectations, but largely due to a weakening dollar

Netflix Inc. had all the wind in its sails that one could hope for in its second-quarter earnings - they came in well ahead of analysts' expectations for net profit, benefitting strongly from a weakening dollar.

Yet Netflix shares (NFLX) fell in after-hours trading. After recently reaching record highs, some analysts are wondering if the stock has gotten ahead of the company's ambitious growth plans.

"Given the run-up in the equity leading up to today's release, we are not surprised to see shares trading modestly lower in after-hours trading," Jason Bazinet of Citi Research wrote in a note to clients.

Netflix executives were quick to point out that their strong numbers in the quarter weren't entirely driven by currency exchange, but "strength in our underlying business."

"We are seeing healthy member growth ... and solid ad sales," Netflix's Chief Financial Officer Spencer Neumann said in a call with analysts.

The streaming giant reported net income of $3.13 billion, compared to $2.15 billion in the same quarter a year earlier. Analysts had estimated net income of $3.05 billion, according FactSet.

Earnings per share came in at $7.19, the company reported. Analysts polled by FactSet had predicted EPS of $7.07.

Netflix shares closed up 1.9% on Thursday, ahead of the release of the second-quarter results, but lost steam in the after-hours session, dropping about 1%. The company's stock hit a record high of $1,341.15 on June 30, rebounding from trading as low as $855 at the beginning of April.

Revenue rose to $11.08 billion, up from $9.56 billion in the year-ago period. That came in slightly above the $11.06 billion that analysts had predicted.

Netflix updated its 2025 revenue forecast to between $44.8 billion and $45.2 billion, up from the $43.5 billion to $44.5 billion it had forecast previously, primarily due to better foreign-exchange conditions stemming from a weakening dollar, plus the member growth numbers and ad sales.

This was the second quarter since Netflix stopped reporting subscriber numbers, which had previously been considered a key indicator of growth, as the company attempts to transition to more traditional financial metrics as markers of its development.

More recently, the company has moved to drive growth by expanding into new areas, such as live events, and through the development of an advertising-supported subscription tier.

Earlier this year, the Wall Street Journal reported that Netflix had set the ambitious target of doubling its revenue by 2030 and reaching a market capitalization of $1 trillion. The company's current market cap is around $536 billion.

The company also aims to have 400 million global subscribers by then, up from just over 300 million at the end of 2024. Netflix also is aiming to triple its annual operating income by 2030 from around $10.4 billion last year, the Journal reported.

Netflix has typically relied on the strength of its programming, including hit shows like "Adolescence," "Black Mirror" and "Squid Game," to bring in more and more viewers. The recent release of the third season of "Squid Game" set an all-time record for Netflix, having been watched by 106.3 million viewers in its first 10 days.

Ted Sarandos, Netflix's co-CEO, said that while hit shows certainly help accelerate growth, even a smash may deliver just 1% of overall engagement, so the company was seeking ways to be less reliant on highly successful programs.

"It's about a steady drumbeat of programming, and now more sports and live events going forward," he said.

In late 2022, Netflix added a lower-cost, ad-supported subscription tier, which has been a slowly building contributor to subscriber growth. Netflix's other co-CEO, Greg Peters, said in the call with analysts that the company had successfully introduced its own advertising tech stack in all of its markets and that early results were good.

"We see good performance metrics across all of our markets," he said.

The lower-priced package - which costs $7.99 a month, versus $24.99 a month for a premium ad-free package - has enabled Netflix to attract a large number of customers who may have been put off by the higher price, analysts have said.

But some analysts have wondered if the company's stock price has gotten ahead of its actual growth, with much of its expansion already priced into its share price but with the actual benefit remaining perhaps years off.

"We just think the company may need some time to execute against expectations before there can be another leg up for the shares," David Joyce at Seaport Research Partners wrote last week.

Analysts polled by Factset came up with an average target share price of $1,270.64, only slightly above where the stock is currently trading.

Laurent Yoon of Bernstein Research wrote that he saw no "compelling reason to be bearish in the foreseeable future to question the underlying fundamentals or the long-term value of the company."

"The bigger question we've been wrestling with is whether Netflix is on a credible path to join the Trillion Dollar Club by 2030," he said.

-Lukas I. Alpert

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Canada sees growing appetite for conversations on Mercosur trade pact, minister says
data.description:
Canada's International Trade Minister said on Thursday that there was an appetite from both sides to advance conversations on a trade deal with South American bloc Mercosur, as Canada seeks trade pacts globally in push to diversify from the U.S.

Prime Minister Mark Carney and his team have been locked in talks with U.S. President Donald Trump to hash out a trade deal by August 1, which could help reduce tariffs on Canada.

But his government is also preparing to rely less on a relationship that generated bilateral trade of over C$1 trillion ($727.33 billion) last year and focusing on diversifying trade by signing free trade pacts globally.

"I had conversations with the foreign minister of Brazil, and there is appetite to carry conversations around Mercosur," , Minister Maninder Sidhu said in an interview with Reuters.

Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva had said in April that he was interested in advancing talks for a trade deal between South America's Mercosur bloc and Canada.

Mercosur - which includes Brazil, Argentina, Paraguay and Uruguay - has had negotiation rounds for a trade deal with Canada in the past.

Canada is also keen to continue talks with China to address trade challenges and sees a thawing of relations between India and Canada as an important step to support trade, the Minister said.

Canada has 15 free-trade deals covering 51 countries, giving it access to 1.5 billion consumers and Sidhu said that Canada will be pursuing more such deals in the coming months without giving a specific target.

data.title: Japan's Underlying Inflation Looks Certain to Overshoot BOJ's Forecasts — Market Talk
data.description: Japan's underlying inflation is still elevated, and is almost certain to overshoot BOJ's forecasts, Capital Economics' Abhijit Surya says in commentary. The country's inflation excluding fresh food and energy rose 3.4% on year in June, up from the 3.3% increase in May, the senior APAC economist notes. "There continues to be a compelling case for the BOJ to resume its tightening cycle later this year," Surya says. However, U.S.-Japan tariff negotiations remain "up in the air," so there's a growing risk that the BOJ will delay its next rate increase until early 2026, Surya adds. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Hankook Tire May Offset U.S. Tariff Costs by Raising Product Prices — Market Talk
data.description: Hankook Tire & Technology will likely be able to offset U.S. tariff costs by raising product prices, Samsung Securities analysts Esther Yim and Hyunzi Kim write in a note. The South Korean tire maker raised prices for its tire products by 5%-8% in the U.S. in July after increasing them by 2%-4% each in Europe and Korea earlier this year, they say. In response to Trump's higher duties, the company is boosting production at its plants in Tennessee, with a new factory set to begin operations in 3Q, the analysts note. They are upbeat on Hankook's improving shareholder returns, with its dividend yield expected to rise to 6.1% this year, up from 5.2% in 2024. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

Investors are focusing on any developments related to U.S. tariffs and domestic economic policy ahead of the Upper House election on Sunday. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 0.6% to 39901.19 on Thursday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1935 ET - Australia's S&P/ASX 200 looks set to round out the week on a high after U.S. indices closed at fresh records. ASX futures are up by 0.6% ahead of Friday's session, suggesting that the benchmark index, which closed Thursday at a record, will add to the 0.7% rise compiled so far this week. Ahead of the open, mining giant BHP reported higher annual iron-ore and copper output, but flagged higher-than-expected costs at its giant Canadian potash operation. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite closed at records after upbeat earnings from some major companies. The S&P added 0.5% and the Nasdaq gained 0.7%. The DJIA rose 0.5%. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1830 ET - Netflix still trusts its playbook. Executives on the streaming service's 2Q investor call note that consolidation of studio and network assets is likely to continue, but they don't think it materially changes the competitive landscape. As for whether they will partake in such consolidation, the executives note they've historically built their own capabilities rather than buy them and believe there is a lot of runway for growth without fundamentally changing that playbook. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1820 ET - Netflix faces competition from a number of platforms for time spent watching content, but it also knows there's a lot of market share left to grab. Executives on the streaming service's 2Q investor call note competition for share of TV time is stiff between traditional cable, other streamers, video games and social media. But they also note that there's about 8% of total TV viewership that neither Netflix or Youtube are winning right now, and that's where the vast majority of its time and money is focused. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1804 ET - Netflix's redesigned user interface was meant to reflect where its platform is today. Executives on the streaming service's 2Q investor call said the previous UI was designed for the Netflix of 10 years ago. The company has drastically evolved since, notably in areas including live events and games. Executives pointed to the fact that last week the platform for the first time held a concurrent pair of live events, a significant step up from its initial foray into live streams that were sometimes marred by glitches and lagging streams. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1758 ET - Netflix isn't seeing any pain points in the economy from its end. Executives on the streaming service's 2Q investor call said while they're watching consumer sentiment in the broader economy, at this time there's nothing significant to note in the metrics and indicators it gets through its business. This comes as Netflix has cracked down on password sharing, built out an ad-supported offering and raised prices on some subscription tiers. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1745 ET - The venture capital industry's main trade group saluted the House passage of three cryptocurrency-related bills. "These bills will enhance the venture industry's ability to back transformative companies and drive lasting economic growth," said Bobby Franklin, president and CEO of National Venture Capital Association. He said the bills, GENIUS Act, the CLARITY Act, and the Anti-CBDC Surveillance State Act, establish a clear regulatory framework for stablecoins and other digital assets. Crypto startups globally raised $4.9 billion via 446 venture-capital deals in the first quarter of this year, according to research from Galaxy. Of those deals about 39% were for companies based in the U.S. ( yuliya.chernova@wsj.com )

1713 ET - U.S. home sales rose in June from May, marking five consecutive months of gains, RE/MAX Holdings reports. Chicago had the highest number of sales in June, jumping 12.9% from the same time last year, the report says. Buyers and sellers in the windy city's market have finally come to terms with the current housing landscape, RE/MAX premier broker Mike Opyd says. The mental shift has pushed more sellers to offer properties on the market for buyers to jump on before prices rise even further, Opyd says. (natalie.weger@wsj.com; @NatalieWeger)

1643 ET - Canadian consumer spending remained solid in 2Q, says Royal Bank of Canada economist Rachel Battaglia, based on anonymized cardholder data from the lender. This adds to a series of secondary and tertiary data that suggest the economy has not fallen off a cliff, as commentators and analysts feared due to President Trump's tariff threats. For the April-to-June period, core retail sales — which strips on automobiles and gasoline — rose 3%, based on activity on RBC credit and debit cards. Core retail sales did contract in June from May, according to RBC data, although Battaglia notes May was a particularly strong month. Quarterly spending growth remains positive in nearly all categories, Battaglia says, led by dining and entertainment. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1639 ET - Netflix's international content is resonating with its users. The streaming service said non-English language series represented more than one-third of all Netflix viewing in the first half of the year. "Even with our 2025 slate being unusually second-half weighted, our members watched over 95 billion hours on Netflix in the first half of the year, a 1% increase year over year," the company said in a letter to shareholders. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

(matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1758 ET - Netflix isn't seeing any pain points in the economy from its end. Executives on the streaming service's 2Q investor call said while they're watching consumer sentiment in the broader economy, at this time there's nothing significant to note in the metrics and indicators it gets through its business. This comes as Netflix has cracked down on password sharing, built out an ad-supported offering and raised prices on some subscription tiers. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1745 ET - The venture capital industry's main trade group saluted the House passage of three cryptocurrency-related bills. "These bills will enhance the venture industry's ability to back transformative companies and drive lasting economic growth," said Bobby Franklin, president and CEO of National Venture Capital Association. He said the bills, GENIUS Act, the CLARITY Act, and the Anti-CBDC Surveillance State Act, establish a clear regulatory framework for stablecoins and other digital assets. Crypto startups globally raised $4.9 billion via 446 venture-capital deals in the first quarter of this year, according to research from Galaxy. Of those deals about 39% were for companies based in the U.S. ( yuliya.chernova@wsj.com )

1713 ET - U.S. home sales rose in June from May, marking five consecutive months of gains, RE/MAX Holdings reports. Chicago had the highest number of sales in June, jumping 12.9% from the same time last year, the report says. Buyers and sellers in the windy city's market have finally come to terms with the current housing landscape, RE/MAX premier broker Mike Opyd says. The mental shift has pushed more sellers to offer properties on the market for buyers to jump on before prices rise even further, Opyd says. (natalie.weger@wsj.com; @NatalieWeger)

1643 ET - Canadian consumer spending remained solid in 2Q, says Royal Bank of Canada economist Rachel Battaglia, based on anonymized cardholder data from the lender. This adds to a series of secondary and tertiary data that suggest the economy has not fallen off a cliff, as commentators and analysts feared due to President Trump's tariff threats. For the April-to-June period, core retail sales — which strips on automobiles and gasoline — rose 3%, based on activity on RBC credit and debit cards. Core retail sales did contract in June from May, according to RBC data, although Battaglia notes May was a particularly strong month. Quarterly spending growth remains positive in nearly all categories, Battaglia says, led by dining and entertainment. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1634 ET - Netflix can thank a weaker dollar for its raised outlook. The streaming service raised its revenue forecast to $44.8 billion to $45.2 billion for the year, adding that the majority of the increase is due to the recent depreciation of the dollar against most other currencies. Momentum in member growth and ad sales rounds out its higher guidance. Shares tick down 0.5% in after hours trading. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1612 ET - Bank of Nova Scotia's economics team reaffirms its call the Bank of Canada is done cutting rates for this year. Economic activity in Canada looks to be stronger — although still tepid — than what Scotiabank previously believed. It now believes Canada GDP in 2Q to be positive, versus an earlier call of a 1% contraction, citing strong labor data for June and three-straight months of higher existing-home sales. The Scotiabank economics team adds that informal conversations with bank clients suggest a more optimistic view of the economy relative to earlier this year. Scotiabank says the combination of "stronger, but not strong," growth and elevated core inflation will keep the BOC's policy rate steady at 2.75%. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1534 ET - Treasury yields rise as U.S. data indicate layoffs aren't picking up while retail sales remain robust. The indicators weaken the case for fast interest rate cuts by the Fed, as the Trump administration has advocated. The preliminary University of Michigan consumer survey for July, due tomorrow, is expected to tick higher in a WSJ poll, in another potential argument for a longer Fed hold. June housing starts are also forecast to accelerate. The 10-year rises 0.008 percentage point to 4.462% and the two-year increases 0.032 p.p. to 3.916%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1526 ET - Lasting uncertainty is likely to hit U.S. corporate earnings, says Dan Genter, of Genter Capital. He expects an average earnings growth of 9.6% for the S&P 500 names this year, falling to 6.5% in 2026. Genter expects tariffs to have just a temporary impact on inflation, but to be constantly negotiated and keep some uncertainty in the air. The new environment has also made his firm's fixed-income strategy change from a concentration in short and long maturities to one more focused in the belly of the curve, "in the six-, seven-year area," he says. Genter also worries President Trump's tax-and-spending bill could hurt healthcare municipal bonds. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

EVZ expects its fiscal 2025 revenue to be in the range of AU$108 million to AU$110 million and its earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization to range from AU$5.2 million to AU$5.4 million.

Golden State Mining

Golden State Mining said it is reviewing several assets based on technical merit, short-term drilling feasibility, and low sovereign risk, with a focus on projects in Western Australia, in addition to ongoing exploration planning and activities.

S2 Resources found a zone of gold-arsenic-antimony anomalism of up to 0.8 grams per tonne grade of gold with the receipt of assays from the three wide-spaced reconnaissance air-core drilling traverses at the Fosterville project in Victoria.

Mount Hope Mining

Mount Hope Mining received all regulatory approvals for the maiden drilling program at its Mt Hope project in New South Wales, allowing for exploration across three priority targets.

data.title: Select Harvests Target Price Cut 17% to A$4.02/Share by Wilsons>SHV.AU
Business Confidence in Australia Rises in Q2 Despite Easing Conditions
data.description:
Business confidence in Australia lifted in the second quarter despite softer conditions, weaker employment, and ongoing pressure on profitability, according to a survey by the National Australia Bank (NAB) published Thursday.

The NAB business confidence index ticked up 3 points to negative 1 index point, its highest level since late 2022, driven by gains in mining, finance, construction, and transport, despite continued weakness in retail and wholesale.

Meanwhile, the NAB business conditions index eased 1 point to nil as trading conditions and employment softened slightly, and profitability remained in negative territory.

Business conditions fell sharply in finance and retail, while mining saw a strong rebound alongside transport, business services, and property gains, per the report. Retail, manufacturing, and wholesale remained the weakest sectors.

Business conditions eased in New South Wales, fell slightly in Queensland and South Australia, and improved in Victoria and Western Australia, though both stayed negative. Conditions remain weakest in South Australia and Victoria, and strongest in Queensland.

Leading indicators showed modest gains with expected business conditions, forward orders, and capex plans rising, capacity utilisation falling, and output constraints easing. In contrast, labor constraints remain a minor issue by historical standards, the report added.

Cost growth remained steady with a small rise in labour costs and a slight drop in purchase costs; retail price growth slowed to its lowest since early 2021, while wage costs and margin pressures continued to weigh on business confidence.

data.title: Nikkei Gains 0.2%, Led by Tech, Financial Stocks — Market Talk
data.description: Japanese stocks are higher following signs of U.S. economic strength. Tech and financial stocks are leading gains. Nomura Research Institute is up 1.0% and Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group is 0.6% higher. USD/JPY is at 148.47, compared with 148.73 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments related to U.S. tariffs and domestic economic policy ahead of the Upper House elections on Sunday. The Nikkei Stock Average is up 0.2% at 39968.38. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

data.title: Ongoing Securities Investigation Into BellRing Brands, Inc. (BRBR) - Contact Levi & Korsinsky
data.description:
NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / July 17, 2025 / Levi & Korsinsky notifies investors that it has commenced an investigation of BellRing Brands, Inc. ("BellRing Brands, Inc.") concerning possible violations of federal securities laws.

On May 6, 2025, BellRing Brands during its second quarter of 2025 earnings call revealed that certain customers were now choosing to "optimize" their inventories by lowering "their weeks of supply on hand," which would slow sales growth in the third quarter to "low-single-digits."

Following this news, the price of BellRing Brands fell by nearly 19% on the same day. To obtain additional information, go to:

https://zlk.com/pslra-1/bellring-brands-inc-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=157129&wire=1&utm_campaign=50

or contact Joseph E. Levi, Esq.

WHY LEVI & KORSINSKY: Over the past 20 years, Levi & Korsinsky LLP has established itself as a nationally-recognized securities litigation firm that has secured hundreds of millions of dollars for aggrieved shareholders and built a track record of winning high-stakes cases. The firm has extensive expertise representing investors in complex securities litigation and a team of over 70 employees to serve our clients. For seven years in a row, Levi & Korsinsky has ranked in ISS Securities Class Action Services' Top 50 Report as one of the top securities litigation firms in the United States. Attorney Advertising. Prior results do not guarantee similar outcomes.

data.title: Investors Urged to Contact Levi & Korsinsky for Information Before August 29, 2025 – Petco Health and Wellness Company, Inc. (WOOF)
data.description:
NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / July 17, 2025 / If you suffered a loss on your Petco Health and Wellness Company, Inc. investment and want to learn about a potential recovery under the federal securities laws, follow the link below for more information:

https://zlk.com/pslra-1/petco-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=157127&wire=1&utm_campaign=5

or contact Joseph E. Levi, Esq. via email at jlevi@levikorsinsky.com or call (212) 363-7500 to speak to our team of experienced shareholder advocates.

THE LAWSUIT: A class action securities lawsuit was filed against Petco Health and Wellness Company, Inc. that seeks to recover losses of shareholders who were adversely affected by alleged securities fraud between January 14, 2021 and June 5, 2025.

CASE DETAILS: (i) Petco's pandemic-related tailwinds were unsustainable, as was its business model of selling primarily premium and/or high-grade pet food; (ii) accordingly, the strength of Petco's differentiated product strategy was overstated; (iii) defendants downplayed the true scope and severity of the foregoing issues, the magnitude of changes needed to rectify those issues, and the likely negative impacts of their mitigation strategy on Petco's comparable sales metric; (iv) accordingly, defendants overstated Petco's ability to deliver sustainable, profitable growth; and (v) as a result, defendants' public statements were materially false and misleading at all relevant times.

WHAT'S NEXT? If you suffered a loss in Petco stock during the relevant time frame - even if you still hold your shares - go to https://zlk.com/pslra-1/petco-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=157127&wire=1&utm_campaign=5 to learn about your rights to seek a recovery. There is no cost or obligation to participate.

WHY LEVI & KORSINSKY: Over the past 20 years, Levi & Korsinsky LLP has established itself as a nationally-recognized securities litigation firm that has secured hundreds of millions of dollars for aggrieved shareholders and built a track record of winning high-stakes cases. The firm has extensive expertise representing investors in complex securities litigation and a team of over 70 employees to serve our clients. data.title: Austrian Privacy Group Lodges Complaint Against China's AliExpress, TikTok, WeChat Over Data Access
data.description:
Austrian privacy advocacy group noyb has lodged data privacy complaints against Alibaba Group's AliExpress, TikTok and Tencent Holdings' WeChat, according to a press release on Thursday.

The complaints allege that the companies have failed to comply with European Union laws regarding user access to their personal data.

The lobby group said TikTok only provided a portion of the complainant's data "in an unstructured form that was impossible to understand," while AliExpress provided a "broken file" that could only be opened once.

It added that Tencent's messaging and social media app WeChat completely ignored the complainant's request.
